mnemonics.title: Page Replacement Algorithms
mnemonics.content: FIFO: First In, First Out - queue-based, oldest page evicted. LRU: Least Recently Used - historical reference tracking. OPTimal: Optimal - future knowledge required, best possible. CLOCK: Second Chance - circular buffer with reference bits.

mnemonics.title: File Allocation Methods
mnemonics.content: Contiguous: Sequential blocks, fast but fragmented. Linked: Scattered blocks with pointers, no fragmentation but slow. Indexed: Index block points to all data blocks, direct access but index overhead.

mnemonics.title: Virtual Memory Benefits
mnemonics.content: MEMORY: M - More programs than physical memory, E - Execution isolation between processes, M - Memory protection, O - Overcommitment possible, R - Relocation easier, Y - Yields larger address space.

mnemonics.title: File Permission Bits
mnemonics.content: Owner-Group-Others (ugo): Read(4), Write(2), Execute(1). Sum values: 7=rwx, 6=rw-, 5=r-x, 4=r--, 0=---. Use chmod 755 for rwxr-xr-x.

acronyms.usage: Technique in fork() where parent and child share pages until modification, then copies are made - saves memory and speeds process creation.
Kernel data structure maintaining file metadata: permissions, size, timestamps, pointers to data blocks, open mode.
Hardware cache storing recent virtual-to-physical address translations, speeds up memory access in paged systems.
Hardware component allowing devices to transfer data to/from memory without CPU intervention, improving I/O performance.
Page replacement algorithm selecting the page with oldest last-reference time - good approximation of optimal algorithm.
Page replacement algorithm replacing oldest page in memory - simple but not always optimal, suffers from Belady's anomaly.

visualTips.visualization: Think of a page table as a dictionary: page number (word) → frame number (translation). Like a book index: topic (page#) → page location (frame#). Multi-level page tables are like chapter indexes pointing to section indexes.
Mount point is like adding a new floor to a building. The root (/) is ground floor; /home, /mnt are new floors added via mount. Each partition becomes accessible at its mount point.

Page fault = librarian looking for a book not on the shelf. Steps: 1) Check if book exists (valid page) 2) Find empty space 3) Get book from storage (disk) 4) Place on shelf (memory) 5) Continue reading (resume process).

Imagine two people sharing a document on a shared drive. Both can read the same copy. When one tries to edit, the system instantly makes a private copy for them - the other continues with the original. Saves paper (memory)!

analogies.analogy: Virtual memory is like a library's off-site storage. The main library (RAM) has limited space, so books (pages) not frequently used are stored elsewhere (disk). When you need an off-site book, the library retrieves it - you don't need to know where it's stored.

Page replacement is like managing a small bookshelf. When full and you need a new book, you must decide which book to remove and return to storage. LRU removes the book you haven't read in longest time; FIFO removes the oldest placed book.

Mounting a USB drive is like adding a new section to a library catalog. The books (files) on the USB become accessible through the library's main catalog at the mount point (/mnt/usb), without reorganizing the entire library.

Directories are like folders in a filing cabinet. Tree structure is hierarchical folders within folders. Acyclic graph is like having the same document appear in multiple folders (hard links) - one physical file, multiple references.

File permissions are like office access cards. Read=can view documents, Write=can modify, Execute=can run programs. Owner sets permissions for different groups - like manager deciding who can view/edit/run files.
